Were You Asked This?
Design Google Docs

Design a collaborative document editing service similar to Google Docs where multiple users can create, edit, and share documents in real-time with concurrent editing capabilities.

Asked at:

Amazon

Netflix

Expedia

Bloomberg

+4 More
Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

Google Docs is a real-time collaborative document editor where multiple people can create, edit, and comment on rich-text documents at the same time. Users see each other's cursors, changes merge automatically, and access is controlled through sharing and permissions.

Interviewers ask this because it forces you to reason about real-time updates, high-contention writes, conflict resolution (OT/CRDT), stateful connection management (WebSockets), storage/versioning, and search with permissions. It reveals whether you can make pragmatic trade-offs under latency, consistency, and scalability constraints, especially for "hot" documents with many concurrent editors.

Expect to clarify scope, define core SLAs (latency, availability, durability), and then drill into collaboration algorithms, transport, sharding, and index/search, while keeping security and cost in check.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
Google Docs

System design answer key for designing a collaborative document editor like Google Docs, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

View Answer Key

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to create, edit, and format documents with multiple collaborators seeing updates in real-time.

Users should be able to share documents with view/comment/edit permissions and manage access.

Users should be able to view version history and revert to a previous version of a document.

Users should be able to search across their documents (title and content) while respecting permissions.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you resolve concurrent edits on the same document (OT vs CRDT), and what consistency/latency trade-offs will you accept?

How do you achieve low-latency real-time fanout and presence for active documents at scale (10–1000 concurrent editors/viewers per doc)?

How do you store documents, deltas, and versions efficiently while enabling quick load and recovery (snapshots, compaction, and hot document caching)?

How will you support search across a user’s documents (title and body) with strict permission filtering and fast query latency?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Collaborative editing depends on sub-second propagation of operations to all participants. You need push-based delivery (WebSockets/SSE), presence, fanout, and replay buffers to keep clients in sync with low latency.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

A single document can become a hot shard with many concurrent writers. You must handle conflicting writes with OT/CRDT, serialize or transform operations, and mitigate hotspots with partitioning and careful leader election.

Read More
Scaling Writes

Edits are frequent, bursty writes. The system must absorb high write rates per doc (operation logs), compact deltas, and scale horizontally by sharding documents while maintaining per-document ordering guarantees.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Redis is well-suited for presence tracking, distributed pub/sub across collaboration servers, and ephemeral state like recent operation buffers or locks. It helps fanout edits and coordinate nodes with low latency.

Read More
Elasticsearch

Elasticsearch provides full-text search with relevance scoring and filtering. It can index document titles and bodies while storing ACL metadata, enabling fast queries that respect permissions for the user.

Read More
PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL is a solid choice for durable metadata (users, documents, ACLs), document snapshots, and an append-only operation log per document. It supports transactions, indexing, and JSON/JSONB for flexible payloads.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

FB Live Comments

Both require real-time fanout to many subscribers, ordering considerations, and handling hot topics/documents with spikes in updates while keeping latency low.

Read
Practice
WhatsApp

Messaging systems share push transport, connection management (WebSockets), presence, and delivery guarantees, which map closely to broadcasting document operations to collaborators.

Read
Practice
Dropbox

File sync involves versioning, conflict resolution, and offline edits. While Dropbox is file-based and Google Docs is operation-based, both need merge strategies, snapshots, and efficient storage of deltas.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Hand-waving concurrent edit resolution without choosing OT or a specific CRDT

Avoiding a concrete algorithm leaves correctness undefined. Without a clear transform/merge strategy, the system cannot guarantee convergence or intention preservation under contention.

Using only periodic polling/REST for real-time collaboration

Polling introduces high latency and wasted load, failing to meet sub-second UX expectations. Real-time editors need push channels (WebSockets/SSE) and coordinated fanout to scale.

Ignoring permissions in search/indexing

Indexing document content without ACL-aware filtering can leak private data. You must propagate ACL changes to the index and enforce authorization at query time to prevent exposure.
